sentences.text: About 11 months, a little bit more, but it's rather hard to-- to know exactly the number of months, but it was a pretty long time.
I know in 1941, I came already to another camp, the camp of [NON-ENGLISH].
How we get there?
By the-- they took us on a truck and they took us another camp.
For it was a few miles away, in the same area, but just another-- another place.
Well, [NON-ENGLISH], that he was already a little bit a better camp.
That was-- we had already water.
And it was a washroom built.
What we had water in the washroom that we could every day take at least cold water to wash up.
Once a week, we had warm water to take a bath.
That camp was then not from the Gestapo, and not from the SS.
It was run by civilian company-- a German civilian company, what was much better already the circumstance.
Was the living conditions was much better.
I would say it was about 50% better than the other camps.
Main thing, you work in a camp and you're giving your blood, your sweat, but at least what they treat you a little bit humane.
And that camp was already a little bit more humane.
Well, in the respect what the-- the food was a little bit better.
In the morning, we got a bigger piece of bread, a coffee.
And the soup when we came home was already a little bit thicker.
It was a little better.
And as I said, the sleeping conditions was better.
It was not bunk beds-- three storeys-- was only two-storey bunk bed.
And was cleaner.
And it was not so-- with the lice and fleas and things like the other camps were.
And on top of that as I mentioned it, what we could take a bath.
Every day when you get up before you go to work, before you take your piece of bread that you can go in the washroom to wash up like a human being.
Because as far I was concerned, to me, that was a much more improvement.
I know that I had no choice.
I had to work.
But for crying out loud, to-- people taken to work, not to give them any the possibility to keep it clean.
But you have to the-- at night with the flies, with the fleas would eat you alive, but you had to go out from the barracks and to sleep outside.
And many times I slept outside in the other camps.
In the middle night, the rains came on you.
You practically drowned because you slept in the air, weather comes with the mountains and you-- you wake up, you know that you're in the flood of water.
But here we didn't have to do that.
So that that is already an improvement.
At this camp, again we're-- we're cutting wood, you know, the trees away to make room for the different roads, to put down different roads over there.
And-- it was also very hard work, but it was-- you know, the foremen were a little bit more lenient.
I could talk to them.
I could bribe them, and do some things in that camp.
How I bribed them, with money, usually.
Money talks everywhere, and I had to steal money from home because the home was still-- you know, even my parents were already living in another house.
But still I could take money from home.
And what I did, actually, in that camp, where I bribed the foremen, they allowed me to go away from work and go to farmers to work during the day, what I can get food from the farmers, you know.
In a Saturday, with the-- I would-- took that food, took it home for my parents.
Because in the city, it was very hard to get food.
You know, the bread-- you had to stay in a line.
And many times you came close already to get the bread and they were out, and didn't got it.
You had to wait for another day where they come another shipment what they will bake.
So it was very hard for my parents, my sister-- you know, at home.
So you see, in that camp, I was able to bribe the foremen, go to work at the-- you know-- to the farmers.
And not for money-- just for to give me flour, give me beans, give me the whatever I could get from them for my work.
And Saturday I had a full sack of food-- I brought it home for my parents.
How far away from your town was this camp?
Well, with the street, to go was about 35 kilometers.

When I went to the mountains what I mentioned before, when I found a way through the mountains to go home, I saved about 50% of-- in other words, it was about 15 to 17 kilometers through the mountains.
It was a tough road.
You know, it wasn't-- I had to climb.
I had to go down and up.
But I saved.
It took me about three, four hours to walk.
Well, our house was a brand new home.
What I mentioned it before, and the Germans, and our house was on the main street.
And all the Jewish people had to go out from the main street to different places to live.
They didn't want us in the main street.
So my parents had to move out.
My father was in another life anymore, but my mother and sisters, they had to go live someplace, you know, in a different neighborhood.
What happened to your father?
My father, when he was running away from the Germans, so they-- Gestapo caught him with the Ukraine SS, and they beat him up so severely, what he hardly could breathe.
So later, they throwed him down from a big hill to a water.
They thought he will drown over there.
But my father was a very strong man.
He swim through that lake, or whatever it was over there.
Well, he came home.
But he was sick, because they moved his heart from place.
They beat him up so severely that he was sick.
He was sick for several months and he died.
No, I was in camp.
But I know.
You see, I had the-- I know exactly what's happening on the outside.
I had contact with the outside.
Even-- no matter where I was, in each camp, I made sure that somehow I got-- I found some people that brought me a news, what's happening on the outside.
So I know exactly what's happening on the outside.
And my father, when he died, you see, at that time I was still coming home on the Saturdays, you see.
But I wasn't-- on that day when he died-- I wasn't home, but on Sunday.
You see, they were waiting for Sunday to bury him.
So I was on his burial.
Yeah, to the funeral.
Because after the funeral, I went back to the camp.
At that camp, we were about seven months.
But at that camp what happened, what-- we know already what it will happen.
You see, the-- the Germans walk away their cities, you know, to the ghettos-- little towns, they put to the bigger city where they made ghettos.
And we know the-- I personally know exactly which city will be evacuated and when.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] As I got to know about four months before our city would be evacuated, exactly the date and everything.
Well, as I've mentioned it before, that I had the contact with the underground and they let me know.
Well, it was Polish name like first names, like John, Robert.
Things, you know-- not-- didn't-- it was everything on a personal basis.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] When I got to know this, at that camp was an older gentleman.
He had four sons in that camp-- a father and four sons.
And I was all the time kept in contact with that other gentleman.
He was a very little man.
He was very, very nice.
And I see to it that one of his sons will be the eldest in the barrack.
You know, what he was saying about barracks, take care of the barracks, sweep the barracks, keep it clean.
And he was the eldest, and keep his father for his helper, so that the father wouldn't have to go out to work the hard labor.
And when I seen the things like that, you know-- I was always working for people, you know, and always, you know, was very beloved and very respected.
But in fact, that father, he told me-- I have four sons here, but I hope you don't mind that I will adopt you as my fifth son.
You know, he-- and he did-- and he meant it.
He didn't say, because, you know, the things what I did.
He meant that with all his heart.
And when I got to know what is happening in every city, so I always made a meeting with him and with this sons, and some other people what they were also, felt responsible.
And I told them what's happening with that city was evacuated, and that so many people was killed and that city, that city.
And our city will be evacuated in that and that day.
So they asked me how do know this?
And as much as I loved them, and I respected them, but we knew that kind of situation, you have such a secret where your life depend on it, you cannot share it with nobody even with your own brother.
Because sometimes a slip of the tongue can kill.
So I told them, don't ask me that question, because you should know if my life is important to you, so don't ask how I know.
So his father right away said, not to even have in mind to ask me anymore anything.
Whatever I say to take it for good words.
And we were sitting for hours.
We hadn't slept the whole night, what we can do in order that everybody should go home to see our parents for the last time.
Because we know what's happening-- what they-- they're taking all the older people, all the sick people, they're killing them.
All of the young people, they're putting them in the ghettos.
We couldn't find a solution.
So I said to them, well, I have faith on the top German men-- what he was in the office, the engineer, the top man, what he was responsible for the whole camp.
That I would go up to him to talk, to say to him, you go to-- I said, there is no choice.
If I will not go home to see my parents, then what is our life worth it?
Nothing.
So I went up to him.
I took one of his sons, the older son, with me.
And when I come in the office, he was very-- he was, like I said before, it's civilian German, nice, you know?
Not the type of German was Nazi, you know-- what he would want to kill us.
He needed us to work.
He treated us with the humane.
And for that, I had my trust in him.
So I told him this and this will happened so and so, that and that happens.
So he asked me, how do you know?
And I told him very frank, if I will tell you from where I know, you, as a German, you have to kill me.
Or you have to give me to the Gestapo.
So I cannot tell you, but I know that's-- that's happen.
So he looked at me and smiled.
I told him the truth.
I didn't beat around the bush.
And he think for a few minutes and he says, come back in a couple of days.
I will give you my answer.
I went back to the whole group and I told them to pray, and in two days, he would give me answer.
We waited two days.
We couldn't sleep.
We just prayed.
We said all kind of important prayers.
I know what you are-- if you know what that means-- that [NON-ENGLISH], and things like that, important prayers.
We said that some good things will come out of it.
Sure enough, two days later, I go up and he has a map on the table put out, and he showed me that he can help me.
But I have to guarantee him with my life that nobody will escape, that everybody who he sent out will come back to the camp.
Because he is responsible for every one of us.
So I told him, I give you my guarantee with my life.
I will not go till the last.
I will go.
And what had happened?
He had to pick up all kind of merchandise, all kind of things for the buildings, for the roads, for the-- where had built bridges, and things like that.
So he had to pick up all kind of things in one place.
If I could-- you know, like-- the camp was-- to make it [INAUDIBLE]-- the camp was right here, our city was here.
He had to pick up in between that note from our city, he had to pick up the merchandise.
So he says, he can send the truck, you know, all around here, where it will go through our city, until-- to pick up and we go back.

Five people can go on the truck, jump down, you know, in the beginning of the city, go to their houses, and a certain time to make exactly that time, there will be a safe place that the truck will come back to pick them up, back to the camp.
And sure enough, that's what we did.
Five people went in the morning, five people in the afternoon to-- go to see their parents.
And how did you pick five people?
You know, where everybody wants to go first, right?
I took everybody's names, and put it in a hat and we're pulling names.
Whoever name come out, that went.
I didn't put in my name because I said I will go the last.
That went, so several weeks, it came other week, before the evacuation.
Eight boys had to go and myself.
And they ran out of merchandise to send the truck.
And he cannot send empty trucks, because he was afraid for himself.
So he told me to wait.
Maybe something would come in that he would be able to send.
We waited.
It was already two days before the evacuation.
I said to the boys, tonight after work, I will not take the soup.
I will not waste any time staying in line for the soup.
I will go from the work straight to the mountains and go home.
If you guys want to go with me, fine.
And I want you to know that at that time, there was the law, if they find a Jew outside the ghetto, outside the city, outside the home, outside the camp, will be shot.
They don't wait for the judge and the jury.
They don't wait to put them on trial.
Right away.
But I didn't care.
I said to my friends over there, if I were not go home and I won't see my mother and my sisters, then you can bury me right here.
And I went.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I can tell you [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] when I came home, my mother, God bless her soul, she waited for me.
She know I come home.
And when I came to the house, I couldn't knock on the door.
The neighbors would heard-- they will call the police right away.
So I just touched the window.
My mother opened the window and she said, [NON-ENGLISH].
That means, thank God, Mendel, that you came home, that I be able to see you for the last time.
She know that.
[PAUSES FOR 16 SECONDS]
So I climbed in the window.
And of course, my sisters, and the children of my sister-- one sister was married.
She had beautiful two boys, gorgeous two nephews.
When my brother-in-law, everybody welcomed me, we were happy to-- that I came home.
My mother got already package-- mine underwear and socks and things, she would I would take with me.
And she give me a cup of coffee.
She give me the cup of coffee, and I was so-- so-- so astound, that I didn't felt the cup of coffee.
The cup of coffee just fell down and broke.
My mother says, OK.
OK.
That's all right.
She gave me another cup of coffee and I could be only an hour and a half at home to go out on the city when it was still dark.
Because we met outside the city on the one place to go back to the camp.
When I was ready to go, and I kissed everybody good-bye, the children were crying.
And my sister said, why don't you stay here.
We'll all go together.
And my mother says, uh-uh.
Let him go back to the camp-- he will survive.
No one from us will.
She could foresee what will happen.
She know exactly what will happen.
And sure enough, we went back to the camp.
Later when the evacuation was, I got one letter from the underground back, and the other letter a day later, from one of my sisters what she wrote.
When they took her to-- with the train, but first they took her to the ghetto.
And from the ghetto, they took her to [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Belzec, over there to the crematorium.
My sisters, everybody from the ghetto.
She had wrote a letter, and she threw that down on a place where the people were working, but she know that they will send me the letter to the camp.
And I got my second letter, and exactly my sister wrote what happened.
The SS-- they took 328 people, made them take off their clothes in the middle of the street, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] men, women, and children.
My mother was between the 328 people-- took them on a truck outside the city, prepared a big hole.
The truck was tipping over and they were shooting with machine guns.
Some of them were wounded, some were killed.
The rest were buried alive. 328 people-- men, women, and children.
I can tell you, knowing my mother as I do, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when they made her to take off their clothes in the middle of the street, she didn't felt any pain.
She didn't felt any pain, because she was an Orthodox woman, with a long dress, old timer.
Well-- you know-- respected in the city.
Or them, they do this.
I know how she felt.
And for that, I cannot forgive them, until the day I die.
[CRYING] Every time I think about it-- and I think about it very often-- it breaks my heart.
It is something so inhumane, so uncalled for it-- to take people what didn't do nothing wrong in their life and to do this to them.
Then I don't know.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I don't know why something like this should have happened.

After that, when I got the letters, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] if I wouldn't have my friends, as I mentioned before, what they were ready to give their lives for me, I would do something to myself because that was something that I could not condone.
I could not go over it.
But they helped me a lot.
They sat with me, day and night.
They didn't let me alone.
And they talked to me for several days, but I couldn't get over it.
Because I was very much heart broken.
I said to them, I don't want to live anymore.
If that's happened-- and I know what's happening to the rest of the family that took them to Belzec or to Treblinka to the crematoriums.
So I did not want to live anymore, but only with my friends, they talked to me and they said if-- to remember, if I do something to myself, what would happen to them?
Because they all looked up to me.
I was the-- [SIGHS]
I don't know what-- how to say this.
The way I did things for everybody, what everybody is-- was with me, for me.
Because I never evaluated my life more than anybody else's.
Or matter of fact, I value my life much less because I did things for other people, and that's what made me happy that I could help other people.
I never took for myself nothing, but only to do something for everybody else.
Well, after that happened, they took us on a place where they were working in the steel-- you know, to pick out some people from us to send them to the ghetto, and they sent us to another camp.
The camp was [NON-ENGLISH].
And at [NON-ENGLISH], we finished the work what we start-- was the same company, actually, but the same company what was before.
And we finished the work, you know, what we started.
And later, they send us to Tarnow to the ghetto.
Tarnow, that is a-- from our city, about 60 kilometers from the-- from our city.
It was a big city.
And over there, they had two ghettos-- they had ghetto A and ghetto B.
With trucks.
They put us on trucks and they transferred us.
That was 19-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 1941-- end of 1941.
Yes, it was late in the year.
It was maybe like October.
It was later in the years, but they sent us to the Tarnow ghetto.
And the Tarnow ghetto, as you see what I mentioned it to you, was ghetto A, ghetto B.
Ghetto A was for all the old people, or the sick people.
Older people, you know, what the--
